text:
This paper introduces Graph of Thoughts (GoT), a novel prompting framework that enhances the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) by modeling their deliberation as a flexible graph structure. While existing paradigms like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Tree of Thoughts (ToT) restrict LLM operations to linear sequences or rigid branching structures, GoT enables more complex thought transformations. Specifically, it allows for the aggregation of multiple reasoning paths, the creation of feedback loops for iterative refinement, and the synthesis of disparate units of information (vertices) based on their logical dependencies (edges). 

Through a modular architecture consisting of a Controller, Prompter, and Parser, GoT provides fine-grained control over individual "thoughts," facilitating the decomposition and consolidation of complex problems. Evaluation across several benchmarks—including sorting, set operations, and document merging—demonstrates that GoT significantly outperforms state-of-the-art prompting methods. For instance, in sorting tasks, GoT improves output quality by 62% over ToT while reducing computational costs by more than 31%. To characterize the structural advantages of this approach, the paper also proposes "thought volume" as a new metric, illustrating how graph-based reasoning maximizes the informational reach of LLM outputs. This framework moves LLM prompting closer to the non-linear, networked mechanisms found in human cognition and algorithmic execution.
